class_schedule.topic: What Managers Do: From Taylor to Mintzberg
learning_objectives: Define management and explain the four core functions: planning, organizing, leading, and controlling
Distinguish between efficiency and effectiveness and explain why both matter for organizational success
Identify the ten managerial roles described by Henry Mintzberg and group them into interpersonal, informational, and decisional categories
Explain the three levels of management (top, middle, first-line) and the skill mix each requires according to Robert Katz
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Management and Organizations
Mintzberg, 'The Manager's Job: Folklore and Fact,' Harvard Business Review (1975, reprinted 1990)
Drucker, The Practice of Management (1954), Ch. 1-2 - The Role of Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Every organization you will work for, build, or buy from is held together by managers. The factory line that produces your iPhone, the surgical team that operates on your grandmother, the platoon that secures a hostile village, and the two-person startup that ships its first product all share one feature: someone is responsible for getting work done through other people. That person is a manager. This course is about what they actually do, why some succeed where others fail, and how the discipline of management evolved from a set of intuitive practices into a body of theory and evidence. We start by stripping away the romance. Management is not about giving orders from a corner office. It is messy, interruption-driven, and political. Henry Mintzberg's seminal 1973 study followed CEOs for weeks and found their average activity lasted nine minutes. The classical view that managers calmly plan, organize, lead, and control turned out to be partly fiction. Real managers are firefighters, negotiators, figureheads, and information brokers, often all in the same hour. Understanding the gap between the textbook description of management and the empirical reality is the first step toward becoming an effective manager rather than a frustrated one.
key_concepts: Management defined: the process of coordinating and overseeing the work of others so that organizational goals can be accomplished efficiently and effectively; efficiency means doing things right (using resources wisely), while effectiveness means doing the right things (achieving goals)

The four functions (POLC): planning sets goals and the means to achieve them; organizing arranges work and resources; leading motivates and directs people; controlling monitors performance and corrects deviations; these functions are not sequential but overlap continuously

Mintzberg's ten managerial roles: interpersonal (figurehead, leader, liaison), informational (monitor, disseminator, spokesperson), and decisional (entrepreneur, disturbance handler, resource allocator, negotiator); a manager moves among these roles in rapid succession

Levels of management: top managers (set strategy, examples: CEO, COO, CFO), middle managers (translate strategy into tactics, examples: regional manager, plant manager), first-line managers (supervise non-managerial employees, examples: shift supervisor, team lead)

Katz's three skills: technical skills (knowledge of specific work, dominant at first-line level), human skills (ability to work with people, important at all levels), conceptual skills (ability to think abstractly about complex situations, dominant at top level)

Universal vs contingent management: universalists like Fayol believed there are general principles that apply everywhere; contingency theorists argue the right management approach depends on the situation, including organizational size, technology, environment, and culture

The reality of managerial work: brief, varied, fragmented, oral, action-oriented, and political; most managerial time is spent in unscheduled meetings and communication, not solitary planning, contradicting the classical image of the contemplative executive

Why study management: even if you never hold the title, you will work with managers, be managed, and almost certainly need to coordinate work with others; understanding management makes you more effective in any role and prepares you for leadership when it arrives
lecture_notes.demonstration: Watch a 12-minute clip of Sundar Pichai's typical day at Google (compiled from Bloomberg's CEO profiles). Pause at each activity transition and classify what he is doing into Mintzberg's ten roles. Note how often he switches roles, the brevity of activities, and the high proportion of time spent in interpersonal and informational roles versus solitary planning. Compare this to what students imagined a CEO does before watching.
setup_requirements: Robbins & Coulter Management textbook (15th edition) — chapter 1
Access to Bloomberg or Wall Street Journal CEO profile videos

review.value: An organization can be efficient (low waste) but ineffective (wrong goals) — Kodak ran efficient film factories while the world moved to digital; the goal is both, not one or the other

review.key: POLC is a Frame, Not a Schedule
review.value: Planning, organizing, leading, and controlling describe what managers do over time, but in any given hour a manager may shift among all four; the framework is for analysis, not for time-blocking

First-line managers need deep technical knowledge of the work they supervise, while top executives need conceptual ability to see patterns across the whole organization; promoting the best engineer is not always promoting the best manager
Real managerial work is brief, fragmented, and oral, not the calm planning depicted in textbooks; expecting to spend hours in quiet contemplation is a recipe for disappointment and ineffectiveness

class_schedule.topic: The Evolution of Management Thought: Scientific, Administrative, and Human Relations Schools
learning_objectives: Explain the contributions of Frederick Taylor and scientific management, including time studies and the principle of one best way
Describe Henri Fayol's 14 principles of management and his five elements of administration
Summarize Max Weber's theory of bureaucracy and explain why it was an improvement over its predecessors
Trace the human relations movement from the Hawthorne Studies to the modern emphasis on employee engagement
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 2 - Management History
Taylor, The Principles of Scientific Management (1911), Ch. 1-2 (selected passages)
Mayo, The Human Problems of an Industrial Civilization (1933), summary of the Hawthorne Studies

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern management did not exist before about 1880. Factories were run by foremen who used rule-of-thumb methods, supervised by owners who had little theory of organization. Labor productivity was low, waste was high, and labor relations were often violent. The first systematic attempt to improve this was scientific management, pioneered by Frederick Winslow Taylor at Bethlehem Steel and Midvale Steel. Taylor measured how long each motion of a worker took, redesigned the motions to eliminate waste, and trained workers in the new methods. He paid more for higher output, doubling productivity in some experiments. Taylor's ideas were influential and controversial: they raised wages and outputs but also dehumanized workers and gave management new tools to control labor. Almost simultaneously, Henri Fayol in France was developing a parallel theory focused on the work of senior managers rather than line workers. Max Weber in Germany was analyzing bureaucracy as a rational alternative to favoritism and feudal authority. By the 1930s the Hawthorne Studies at Western Electric had revealed that workers respond to social and psychological factors, not just economic incentives, opening the human relations school. Each tradition lives on inside modern management practice. Scientific management is alive in fast food job design; bureaucracy structures every Fortune 500 HR department; human relations underpins modern engagement surveys. Knowing the history is not academic — it tells you which assumptions you have inherited.
key_concepts: Frederick Taylor and scientific management: use the scientific method to determine the one best way to do each job; scientifically select, train, and develop workers; provide detailed instruction and supervision; divide work between managers (planning) and workers (execution)

Time and motion studies: Taylor and the Gilbreths (Frank and Lillian) decomposed work into elementary motions, measured each, and redesigned jobs to remove waste; the bricklaying study cut motions per brick from 18 to 5 and tripled productivity

Henri Fayol's 14 principles: division of work, authority, discipline, unity of command, unity of direction, subordination of individual interest, remuneration, centralization, scalar chain, order, equity, stability of tenure, initiative, esprit de corps

Fayol's five functions of management: planning, organizing, commanding, coordinating, controlling — the precursor of the modern POLC framework; Fayol focused on the manager's perspective while Taylor focused on the worker's task

Max Weber's bureaucracy: division of labor, hierarchy of authority, formal selection based on technical qualifications, formal rules and regulations, impersonality, career orientation; an alternative to traditional and charismatic authority based on rational-legal authority

The Hawthorne Studies (1924-1932): begun as illumination experiments at Western Electric's Hawthorne plant, they accidentally discovered that productivity rose regardless of lighting changes — workers performed better simply because they were being observed and felt important (the Hawthorne Effect)

Human relations movement: pioneered by Elton Mayo and Mary Parker Follett; argued that organizations are social systems, not just economic ones; productivity depends on group norms, supervisor behavior, and worker satisfaction, not just wages and conditions

Quantitative school (operations research): emerged from World War II logistics and gunnery problems; applies mathematical models to management decisions like inventory control, queueing, and linear programming; modern descendants include operations research and business analytics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Watch the 'Pig Iron' passage from Taylor's Principles of Scientific Management read aloud, where Taylor describes increasing a worker's daily output from 12.5 to 47 tons by redesigning the rest cycle. Discuss as a class: was this exploitation or empowerment? Then watch a clip of the Hawthorne Studies documentary explaining the relay assembly test room. Contrast Taylor's view of workers as economic actors with Mayo's view of workers as social beings, and connect to modern debates about Amazon warehouse productivity tracking.
setup_requirements: Excerpts from Taylor's The Principles of Scientific Management (public domain)
Hawthorne Studies summary documentary or article

review.value: Scientific management raised wages and productivity but also reduced worker autonomy and contributed to labor unrest; modern echoes appear in Amazon warehouses, call centers, and gig work where every motion is timed and tracked

Today bureaucracy is a slur, but Weber proposed it as a rational alternative to nepotism, favoritism, and arbitrary authority; civil service exams and merit-based hiring are bureaucratic ideals worth defending

People change behavior when they know they are being observed; this insight reshaped employee engagement, performance reviews, and even research methodology, where it forces controls to isolate the observer effect
Modern management is not scientific OR human relations — it is both, layered together; McDonald's job design is Taylorist, McDonald's culture and engagement programs are human-relations-driven

learning_objectives: Distinguish between the general environment and the specific (task) environment of an organization
Apply PESTEL analysis to identify political, economic, sociocultural, technological, environmental, and legal forces
Use Porter's Five Forces framework to analyze industry attractiveness
Describe organizational culture and explain how it shapes decision-making, behavior, and performance
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 3 - Managing the External Environment and the Organization's Culture
Porter, 'The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy,' Harvard Business Review (January 2008)
Schein, Organizational Culture and Leadership (5th ed.), Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Managers do not work in a vacuum. Every decision is constrained and shaped by forces outside the organization (regulation, competitors, technology, customer preferences) and inside it (culture, structure, history, politics). Failing to scan the environment is the most common cause of strategic failure. Kodak invented the digital camera in 1975 and shelved it because it threatened the film business; by the time Kodak embraced digital, Canon, Sony, and the smartphone had taken the market. Blockbuster turned down a chance to buy Netflix for $50 million in 2000 and went bankrupt in 2010. Both companies had brilliant managers, deep capabilities, and strong cash flow. What killed them was failing to read the environment. This session gives you the diagnostic tools to avoid that fate. PESTEL forces you to think systematically about six categories of external change. Porter's Five Forces shows you why some industries are profitable for everyone and others starve every entrant. The internal environment, especially culture, often eats strategy for breakfast — Peter Drucker's phrase is now corporate cliché but it remains true. A new strategy that conflicts with the existing culture will lose unless leadership confronts the culture directly.
key_concepts: General environment: broad forces affecting all organizations — political/legal, economic, sociocultural, technological, demographic, global; analyzed using PESTEL or PEST frameworks

Specific (task) environment: forces that directly interact with the organization — customers, suppliers, competitors, regulators, strategic partners; managers can influence the task environment more than the general environment

PESTEL analysis: Political (regulation, tax policy), Economic (interest rates, GDP, inflation), Sociocultural (demographics, lifestyles, values), Technological (innovation, R&D, automation), Environmental (climate, sustainability), Legal (employment law, IP, antitrust)

Porter's Five Forces: threat of new entrants, bargaining power of suppliers, bargaining power of buyers, threat of substitutes, rivalry among existing competitors; each force is a profitability sink or a profitability source

Environmental uncertainty: a function of complexity (number of components in environment) and dynamism (rate of change); high uncertainty environments require more flexible structures and faster information processing

Organizational culture: shared values, beliefs, assumptions, and norms that shape how members behave; Schein's three levels — artifacts (visible), espoused values (stated), basic underlying assumptions (taken for granted)

Strong vs weak cultures: strong cultures have widely shared values and produce consistent behavior, easing coordination but resisting change; weak cultures have inconsistent values and require formal rules to coordinate

Culture types: clan (collaboration, family-like), adhocracy (innovation, dynamic), market (results-oriented, competitive), hierarchy (control, structure) — from the Competing Values Framework by Cameron and Quinn

How culture is formed and sustained: founders impose values; selection and socialization perpetuate them; rituals, stories, language, and physical layout reinforce them; leaders' attention and reactions teach what is important
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a live PESTEL analysis of the U.S. electric vehicle industry as of 2026: political (IRA tax credits, tariffs on Chinese EVs), economic (interest rates affecting auto loans), sociocultural (changing attitudes toward car ownership, range anxiety), technological (battery chemistry, charging infrastructure), environmental (climate policy, lifecycle emissions), legal (NHTSA safety standards, dealer franchise laws). Then apply Porter's Five Forces to Tesla specifically, identifying which forces are favorable and which are mounting against it.
setup_requirements: Recent industry analyst reports on the EV market (S&P, Gartner, or McKinsey)

review.value: PESTEL is not a once-a-year exercise; the most successful firms monitor environmental signals constantly so they can respond to weak signals before competitors notice

Porter's framework explains why airlines collectively lose money over time (low entry barriers, powerful suppliers, low switching costs) while software firms collectively earn high returns (high switching costs, weak supplier power, network effects)
You cannot change culture by issuing a memo; culture changes slowly through changes in selection, leadership behavior, what gets rewarded, and what gets punished — usually over years, not quarters

review.key: Strong Culture Can Be a Liability
review.value: A strong culture coordinates without rules but resists change; IBM's mainframe culture nearly killed it in the 1990s; Microsoft's stack-ranking culture undermined collaboration until Nadella dismantled it

learning_objectives: Distinguish between the classical and socioeconomic views of corporate social responsibility
Compare four approaches to ethical decision-making: utilitarian, rights, justice, and integrative social contracts
Identify the factors that influence ethical and unethical behavior in organizations
Evaluate the stakeholder theory of the firm and apply it to a corporate decision
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 5 - Managing Social Responsibility and Ethics
Friedman, 'The Social Responsibility of Business Is to Increase Its Profits,' New York Times Magazine (September 13, 1970)
Freeman, Strategic Management: A Stakeholder Approach (1984), Ch. 1 - Introduction to Stakeholder Theory

lecture_notes.introduction: Should a manager fire 200 workers to raise next quarter's earnings per share by a penny? Should a clothing brand source from a factory in Bangladesh that pays sub-living wages but employs thousands who would otherwise starve? Should a tech company sell facial recognition to a police department that may misuse it? These are not theoretical exercises. They land on real desks, and the answers given by real managers shape lives, communities, and the long-term reputation of the firm. This session is about the frameworks managers use — explicitly or implicitly — when they answer these questions. The history of corporate ethics is a swing between two poles. Milton Friedman's 1970 article argued that the social responsibility of business is to increase its profits, period; managers who divert corporate funds to social causes are stealing from shareholders. Edward Freeman's stakeholder theory, developed in the 1980s, argued that corporations have obligations to all parties whose interests are affected: employees, customers, suppliers, communities, the environment, and shareholders. Today the pendulum has swung toward stakeholder theory, formalized in the 2019 Business Roundtable statement and the rise of ESG investing. But the underlying tensions remain. Doing the right thing often costs money; not doing the right thing often costs more in the long run. Managers need ethical frameworks to navigate the trade-offs.
key_concepts: Classical view of CSR (Friedman): managers are agents of shareholders; their only social responsibility is to maximize shareholder wealth within the rules of law and ethical custom; spending corporate money on social causes is a form of taxation without representation

Socioeconomic view: corporations have responsibilities beyond profit, including obligations to employees, customers, communities, and the environment; long-term shareholder value depends on social legitimacy
Stakeholder theory (Freeman): the firm should be managed for the benefit of all stakeholders, not just shareholders; stakeholders are any group affected by or affecting the firm's actions

Four ethical decision frameworks: utilitarian (greatest good for greatest number), rights (respect fundamental human rights), justice (fair distribution of benefits and burdens), integrative social contracts (combine local norms with universal principles)

Carroll's pyramid of CSR: economic responsibility (be profitable), legal responsibility (obey the law), ethical responsibility (do what is right), philanthropic responsibility (be a good corporate citizen) — built bottom-up

Influences on ethical behavior: stage of moral development (Kohlberg's six stages), individual characteristics (values, ego strength, locus of control), structural variables (rules, codes, performance pressure), organizational culture, issue intensity

Whistleblowing: reporting wrongdoing to an authority; whistleblowers often face retaliation despite legal protections (Sarbanes-Oxley, Dodd-Frank); ethical organizations build channels that make whistleblowing unnecessary

ESG investing: environmental, social, and governance factors used by investors to evaluate companies beyond financial metrics; assets under ESG management exceeded $40 trillion globally by 2022, though greenwashing is a persistent problem

Code of ethics: a formal statement of an organization's values and ethical rules; effective codes are specific, enforced consistently, and modeled by senior leaders; ineffective codes are vague, ignored, or used selectively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Volkswagen diesel emissions scandal (Dieselgate) of 2015. Engineers installed defeat-device software in 11 million diesel vehicles to cheat emissions tests. Decision tree: who knew, when did they know, why did they go along, what were the alternatives? Apply each of the four ethical frameworks to the decision to install the software, and again to the decision by individual engineers to comply with the order. Conclude with the costs to VW: $30+ billion in fines and settlements, criminal indictments of executives, and a multi-year reputation hit that pushed them toward EV transformation faster than competitors.
setup_requirements: Volkswagen Dieselgate case background (Reuters or NYT investigative pieces)

review.value: Following the law is the floor, not the ceiling; many unethical actions are perfectly legal, and some legal requirements (like Jim Crow laws historically) are deeply unethical
Stakeholder theory does not ask managers to ignore profits; it argues that long-term profits depend on healthy relationships with employees, customers, suppliers, and communities
Most unethical behavior comes from ordinary people in flawed structures, not bad apples; codes of ethics fail when performance pressure rewards rule-breaking and senior leaders look the other way
A reputation for ethical behavior is worth real money — customers pay more, employees accept lower wages, regulators give benefit of the doubt; reputation is destroyed in days and rebuilt in decades

class_schedule.topic: Decision Making: Rational, Bounded Rational, and Intuitive
learning_objectives: Describe the eight steps of the rational decision-making process
Explain bounded rationality and the concept of satisficing as developed by Herbert Simon
Identify common decision biases including anchoring, availability, confirmation, and overconfidence
Distinguish between programmed and nonprogrammed decisions and match decision style to problem type
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 6 - Decision Making
Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (2011), Ch. 1-3 - The Two Systems
Simon, Administrative Behavior (4th ed., 1997), Ch. 5 - The Psychology of Administrative Decisions

lecture_notes.introduction: Management is the process of making decisions and ensuring they are carried out. Strip away everything else — the org chart, the strategy, the meetings — and what remains is a continuous stream of choices: who to hire, what to build, where to invest, when to cut, who to fire. The quality of these decisions, aggregated over time, determines whether an organization thrives or dies. The classical model of decision-making says managers identify problems, gather information, generate alternatives, evaluate them against clear criteria, and choose the optimal one. Herbert Simon won a Nobel Prize for showing this is a fantasy. Real decision-makers face limited time, limited information, and limited cognitive capacity. They satisfice — they choose the first option that meets minimum standards rather than searching for the optimal one. Daniel Kahneman, another Nobel laureate, showed that human cognition uses two systems: a fast, intuitive, automatic System 1 and a slow, deliberate, effortful System 2. System 1 is fast and usually right, but it produces predictable errors when patterns mislead it. The biases Kahneman cataloged — anchoring, availability, representativeness, overconfidence — are not random mistakes but systematic distortions that affect everyone, including experts. Knowing this lets you build decision processes that compensate for human limitations rather than pretending they don't exist.
key_concepts: The rational decision-making process: identify the problem, identify decision criteria, weight the criteria, develop alternatives, analyze alternatives, select an alternative, implement the alternative, evaluate decision effectiveness — eight idealized steps

Assumptions of rationality: the problem is clear and unambiguous, all alternatives and consequences are known, criteria and preferences are clear and stable, there are no time or cost constraints, the decision will maximize the outcome

Bounded rationality (Simon): decision-makers act rationally within the limits of their cognitive ability, time, and information; they satisfice rather than maximize, choosing the first option that is good enough

Escalation of commitment: increasing commitment to a previous decision despite evidence it is failing, often to avoid admitting the original decision was wrong; explains why managers throw good money after bad

System 1 and System 2 (Kahneman): System 1 is fast, automatic, emotional, and intuitive; System 2 is slow, deliberate, logical, and effortful; most decisions are made by System 1, with System 2 only engaging when prompted

Cognitive biases: anchoring (over-rely on first information), availability (overweight memorable examples), representativeness (judge by similarity to stereotype), confirmation (seek confirming evidence), overconfidence (overestimate own knowledge), framing (decisions change based on how options are presented)

Programmed vs nonprogrammed decisions: programmed decisions are routine and handled by rules or procedures (when to reorder inventory); nonprogrammed decisions are novel, ambiguous, and require unique solutions (entering a new market)

Decision conditions: certainty (outcomes known), risk (probabilities known), uncertainty (probabilities unknown); managers prefer certainty but rarely have it; risk and uncertainty are the normal conditions of management

Decision-making styles: directive (low tolerance for ambiguity, rational), analytic (high tolerance for ambiguity, rational), conceptual (high tolerance, intuitive), behavioral (concerned with people)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Run an in-class exercise: ask half the students 'Did Gandhi die before or after age 144?' and the other half 'Did Gandhi die before or after age 35?' Then ask both groups to estimate Gandhi's age at death. The first group's estimates will be systematically higher than the second group's, demonstrating anchoring even on absurd numbers. Then walk through the Bay of Pigs invasion (1961) as a case of groupthink and escalation of commitment — Kennedy and his advisors made a decision they knew was likely to fail and could not back out without admitting the error.
setup_requirements: Anchoring exercise materials (two slides with different anchors)

review.key: You Are Not as Rational as You Think
review.value: Decades of research show that even experts fall for anchoring, availability, and overconfidence; the best decision-makers don't avoid biases but build processes that catch them
Optimizing every decision is impossible and counterproductive; choosing the first option that meets your standards saves cognitive resources for the few decisions where optimization matters
Sunk costs should not influence future decisions, but humans are wired to honor them; the question is not 'how much have I spent' but 'is the next dollar worth the next return'
Use intuition for low-stakes, routine decisions where you have deep experience; use deliberate analysis for high-stakes, novel decisions where intuition has not been trained

class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Planning and Goal Setting
learning_objectives: Distinguish between strategic, tactical, and operational plans and explain their time horizons and audiences
Apply the SMART framework to evaluate and improve goal statements
Compare traditional goal-setting with management by objectives (MBO) and OKRs
Analyze the strengths and weaknesses of formal planning in turbulent environments
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 8 - Foundations of Planning
Drucker, The Practice of Management (1954), Ch. 11 - Management by Objectives and Self-Control
Doerr, Measure What Matters (2018), Ch. 1-3 - The OKR Framework

lecture_notes.introduction: Planning is the most criticized of the four management functions. Critics point out that elaborate plans are usually wrong, get out of date the day they are published, and create false confidence. Yet every well-managed organization plans, and planning has been shown in dozens of studies to correlate with performance. The resolution of this paradox is that the value of planning lies not in the document but in the process. The act of forcing managers to articulate what they are trying to achieve, why, and how, surfaces assumptions, exposes contradictions, and aligns people. The Eisenhower quote — 'Plans are worthless, but planning is everything' — captures this. This session covers the basic mechanics: what kinds of plans organizations make, what good goals look like, and how to deal with the perennial complaint that plans are obsolete the moment they meet reality. We focus heavily on goal setting because clear goals are the most underrated lever in management. The SMART framework (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) sounds like a cliché but is consistently violated in practice. We also examine the modern descendants of MBO: OKRs (Objectives and Key Results), popularized by Andy Grove at Intel and now used by Google, LinkedIn, and many startups. OKRs work because they force ambition (objectives are aspirational), measurement (key results are quantitative), and transparency (everyone sees everyone's OKRs).
key_concepts: Strategic plans: organization-wide, set by top management, cover long time periods (3-5 years), establish overall objectives and position in the environment
Tactical plans: focused on specific functional areas, cover medium time periods (1-3 years), translate strategic goals into department actions
Operational plans: detailed plans for day-to-day activities, set by first-line managers, cover short time periods (weekly, monthly, quarterly)
SMART goals: Specific (clear and unambiguous), Measurable (quantifiable progress), Achievable (challenging but realistic), Relevant (aligned with broader objectives), Time-bound (clear deadline)
Traditional goal setting: top management sets organizational goals, which cascade down through the hierarchy; each level translates higher-level goals into more specific ones for their unit
Management by objectives (MBO): joint goal setting between manager and employee, with periodic reviews of progress and rewards based on goal achievement; introduced by Drucker in 1954

OKRs (Objectives and Key Results): aspirational objectives (qualitative, motivating) paired with 3-5 measurable key results (quantitative); typically set quarterly and graded 0.0-1.0; ambitious targets where 0.7 is success
Formal vs informal planning: formal planning is written, has specific time frames, and is shared throughout the organization; informal planning exists in the manager's head and is rarely shared
Single-use vs standing plans: single-use plans address a one-time situation (a product launch); standing plans (policies, procedures, rules) provide guidance for repeated activities

Criticisms of formal planning: planning creates rigidity, formal plans cannot replace intuition and creativity, planning focuses managers on today's competition rather than tomorrow's survival, formal planning reinforces success which may lead to failure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare three goal statements for an e-commerce startup: (1) 'Increase sales' — vague and unmeasurable; (2) 'Increase sales by 20% by year-end through better marketing' — better but missing specifics; (3) 'Increase North America D2C revenue from $4.2M to $5.5M by Q4 2026 by launching paid social campaigns on TikTok and Instagram with $400K total budget and a target CAC of $35' — specific, measurable, has a how. Then show a real OKR set from Google's early years (the original Larry Page OKRs Doerr quotes in his book) and discuss how the OKR format forced ambition by separating qualitative objectives from quantitative key results.

review.key: Planning Is About Process, Not the Document
review.value: The plan will be wrong; the value comes from the conversations and alignment that produce it; running planning as a paperwork exercise misses the point

review.key: Vague Goals Are Worse Than No Goals
review.value: A goal you cannot measure is a wish; vague goals let everyone interpret success differently and produce conflict at review time

review.key: OKRs Force Ambition by Decoupling Reward
review.value: OKRs work because ambitious targets are explicitly NOT compensation goals; if missing 30% of the target meant losing your bonus, no one would set ambitious targets
In turbulent environments, rolling plans (continuously updated) beat fixed annual plans; the Pentagon's planning process and Amazon's S-team planning both use rolling horizons

class_schedule.topic: Strategic Management: SWOT, Generic Strategies, and Competitive Advantage
learning_objectives: Conduct a SWOT analysis identifying internal strengths and weaknesses and external opportunities and threats
Distinguish between Porter's three generic strategies: cost leadership, differentiation, and focus
Explain the concept of sustainable competitive advantage and identify sources of advantage
Apply the resource-based view (VRIO framework) to assess whether a resource creates competitive advantage
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 9 - Strategic Management
Porter, Competitive Strategy (1980), Ch. 2 - Generic Competitive Strategies
Barney, 'Firm Resources and Sustained Competitive Advantage,' Journal of Management (1991)

lecture_notes.introduction: Strategy answers two questions: where will we compete, and how will we win? The 'where' question is about choosing markets, geographies, customer segments, and product categories. The 'how' question is about choosing the basis of competitive advantage — what makes customers prefer you to alternatives. Most failed strategies are not failures of execution; they are failures to answer these questions clearly. A company that tries to be everything to everyone usually ends up being nothing to anyone. Michael Porter's generic strategies, published in 1980, made this brutally clear. You can win by being the lowest cost producer (Walmart, Costco, Southwest Airlines), by being meaningfully different (Apple, BMW, Whole Foods), or by serving a narrow niche better than anyone else (Ferrari, In-N-Out Burger). Trying to do all three lands you in the middle, where you have neither cost nor differentiation advantage. Sears and JCPenney spent decades stuck in the middle and are now largely gone. The resource-based view, articulated by Jay Barney in 1991, offers a complementary lens. It says competitive advantage comes from resources that are Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, and Organized (VRIO). Apple's brand is valuable, rare, hard to imitate, and exploited through a coordinated organization. That bundle is what produces Apple's industry-leading margins, not any single component.
key_concepts: Strategic management process: identify mission and objectives, analyze external environment, analyze internal environment, formulate strategies, implement strategies, evaluate results — an iterative cycle

SWOT analysis: Strengths (internal positive), Weaknesses (internal negative), Opportunities (external positive), Threats (external negative); used to match internal capabilities with external conditions
Porter's three generic strategies: cost leadership (lowest total cost in industry), differentiation (unique product or service customers value), focus (narrow segment served better than rivals)
Stuck in the middle: firms that fail to achieve either cost leadership or differentiation; usually have lower profitability than firms that commit to one strategy
Sustainable competitive advantage: an advantage that competitors cannot easily duplicate; sustained over time despite competitive responses; the goal of strategy formulation
Resource-based view (RBV): firms are bundles of resources and capabilities; competitive advantage comes from possessing resources that are valuable, rare, hard to imitate, and well organized
VRIO framework: Valuable (exploits opportunity or neutralizes threat), Rare (not possessed by many competitors), Inimitable (hard to copy), Organized (firm structured to exploit it)
Core competencies: a firm's combination of resources and capabilities that allow it to produce something customers value at a level competitors cannot match; Hamel and Prahalad, 1990

Blue Ocean strategy (Kim and Mauborgne): rather than competing in saturated 'red oceans,' create new market space ('blue oceans') where competition is irrelevant; Cirque du Soleil reinvented the circus for adults
Business model innovation: changing the underlying logic of how a firm creates and captures value, not just the product; Netflix moved from DVD-by-mail to streaming to original content production
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a SWOT analysis of Tesla as of 2026: strengths (brand, vertical integration, charging network, software-defined vehicles); weaknesses (quality issues, CEO concentration risk, premium pricing); opportunities (global EV transition, energy storage, autonomous driving); threats (Chinese EV competition, IRA changes, traditional automaker EV launches). Then map Tesla against Porter's generic strategies: is it differentiation, focus, or cost leadership? Discuss the tension between Elon Musk's stated cost leadership goal (the Model 2) and Tesla's premium positioning. Finally, apply VRIO to Tesla's Supercharger network: it is valuable, rare, hard to imitate (capital and time), and now being organized for revenue through opening to other manufacturers.

review.value: If your strategy doesn't say what you will NOT do, it isn't a strategy; trying to please every customer segment and every channel is a recipe for stuck-in-the-middle

review.key: Cost and Differentiation Usually Trade Off
review.value: Cost leadership requires standardization, scale, and process discipline; differentiation requires innovation, customization, and brand investment; pursuing both at once usually fails

review.key: Resources Become Advantages Only Through Organization
review.value: Many firms have valuable resources they fail to exploit; the 'O' in VRIO is often the binding constraint; Xerox PARC invented the GUI and the mouse but failed to commercialize them
No advantage lasts forever; even the best moats are eventually filled; strategy is a continuous process, not a one-time formulation

learning_objectives: Identify the six key elements of organizational structure: work specialization, departmentalization, chain of command, span of control, centralization, and formalization
Compare mechanistic and organic organizational structures and explain when each fits
Describe contemporary structures including matrix, project, team-based, and network organizations
Apply contingency factors (strategy, size, technology, environment) to choose an appropriate structure
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 11 - Designing Organizational Structure
Mintzberg, The Structuring of Organizations (1979), Ch. 1-3 - The Five Basic Parts of the Organization
Galbraith, Designing Organizations (2014), Ch. 1-2 - The Star Model

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizational structure is the formal framework by which jobs are grouped, coordinated, and controlled. Get it right and information flows, decisions happen at the right level, and accountability is clear. Get it wrong and meetings multiply, decisions stall, and good people leave in frustration. Most organizations restructure every few years not because the old structure was perfect once and now obsolete, but because no structure perfectly resolves the trade-offs between specialization and coordination, autonomy and control, speed and consistency. There is no universal best structure, only a best structure for a given situation. This session focuses on the six structural elements you can manipulate, the two extreme types (mechanistic and organic), and the contingency factors that determine which extreme you should approach. We also cover contemporary structures that have emerged as organizations respond to faster environments and knowledge work — matrix, team-based, virtual, and network structures. The hard truth is that every structure has costs as well as benefits. A flat structure increases speed but reduces career paths. A matrix structure improves coordination but multiplies bosses. A functional structure deepens expertise but creates silos. The manager's job is to choose the structure whose costs you can live with given the work you need to do.
key_concepts: Work specialization (division of labor): degree to which tasks are subdivided into separate jobs; high specialization increases productivity through expertise but reduces job satisfaction and worker flexibility

Departmentalization: how jobs are grouped — by function (engineering, marketing, finance), product (consumer, enterprise, cloud), geography (Americas, EMEA, Asia-Pacific), customer (small business, mid-market, enterprise), or process (sales, fulfillment, support)
Chain of command: unbroken line of authority from top to bottom; clarifies who reports to whom; modern flat organizations have shorter chains and more peer-to-peer coordination
Span of control: number of subordinates a manager can effectively supervise; wider spans flatten the structure and reduce middle management; narrow spans allow closer supervision but increase costs

Centralization vs decentralization: degree to which decision authority is concentrated at top (centralized) or distributed throughout (decentralized); centralization eases coordination, decentralization speeds response
Formalization: degree to which jobs are standardized through rules, procedures, and job descriptions; high formalization reduces variability but limits adaptability
Mechanistic structure: high specialization, rigid departmentalization, narrow spans, high formalization, centralized authority — fits stable environments and routine work
Organic structure: cross-functional teams, free flow of information, wide spans, low formalization, decentralized — fits dynamic environments and innovative work
Matrix structure: dual chain of command, employees report to both functional manager and project/product manager; improves cross-functional coordination but creates ambiguity and conflict
Network organization: small core firm outsources major business functions; enables flexibility and focus on core competencies but requires strong contract management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two real org structures: McDonald's (mechanistic — high formalization, narrow spans at store level, centralized menu and brand decisions, decentralized franchise operations) and IDEO (organic — project teams, fluid roles, low formalization, decentralized creative authority). Discuss why each structure fits its work: McDonald's needs absolute consistency across 40,000 locations; IDEO needs creativity and flexibility for novel design problems. Then walk through Spotify's 'squad/tribe/chapter/guild' model and how it tries to combine the autonomy of small teams with cross-team alignment.
setup_requirements: Sample org charts (functional, divisional, matrix, network)

review.value: Alfred Chandler's classic finding (1962): firms that change strategy without changing structure underperform; the structure that worked when you were a single-product firm won't work when you are a diversified conglomerate
Wider spans save management costs and push decisions down, but they require more capable and self-directed employees; narrow spans allow close supervision but slow decisions and add layers

Matrix organizations promise cross-functional coordination but deliver dual reporting, unclear accountability, and political fights; they work only with strong conflict-resolution norms and senior leadership commitment

Reorgs disrupt informal networks, anger customers and employees, and rarely deliver promised gains in the short term; restructure only when the existing structure is clearly failing, not as a substitute for harder strategic work

learning_objectives: Describe the HRM process from job analysis through separation
Identify the legal context affecting HR decisions including key US employment laws
Compare recruitment sources and selection devices and assess their reliability and validity
Explain the role of training, performance management, and compensation in employee development
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 12 - Managing Human Resources
Dessler, Human Resource Management (16th ed.), Ch. 4 - Job Analysis and the Talent Management Process
Cascio & Aguinis, Applied Psychology in Talent Management (8th ed.), Ch. 9 - Selection Methods

lecture_notes.introduction: People are the most valuable and the most expensive resource in most modern organizations. For knowledge-intensive firms — software, consulting, finance, biotech, healthcare — people-related costs are 60-80% of total expenses, and the quality of people determines success more than any other factor. HRM is the function that sources, develops, and retains those people, and it has evolved from a clerical activity in the 1950s to a strategic discipline today. This session covers the foundations every general manager needs to understand, even if you never work in HR. You will participate in hiring decisions, design jobs for your team, give performance feedback, and recommend compensation actions. The legal context of HR is unusually heavy in the United States, where employment is subject to federal laws (Title VII, ADA, ADEA, FLSA, FMLA), state laws (California's are typically the most protective), and a thicket of court rulings. Ignorance of this context is not a defense; managers who treat employees in ways that violate employment law expose their firms to lawsuits and regulatory penalties. We focus on the most critical decisions: how to write a job description that actually describes the job, where to find candidates, how to conduct interviews that predict performance (most don't), and how to manage performance in ways that develop people rather than demoralize them.
key_concepts: HRM process: human resource planning, recruitment and decruitment, selection, orientation, training, performance management, compensation and benefits, career development, separation
Job analysis: systematic process of determining what work is performed in a job; produces job descriptions (duties) and job specifications (qualifications); foundation of all other HR activities

Key US employment laws: Title VII (1964) prohibits discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, national origin; ADA (1990) protects qualified individuals with disabilities; ADEA (1967) protects workers 40+; FLSA sets minimum wage and overtime; FMLA provides unpaid leave

Recruitment sources: internal (promotions, transfers), employee referrals, employment agencies, job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed), college campuses, professional associations; each has different cost, speed, and quality trade-offs

Selection devices: application forms, written tests (cognitive ability, personality), work sample tests, interviews (structured vs unstructured), background checks, drug tests, medical exams (post-offer only under ADA)

Reliability and validity: reliability is consistency of measurement (does the test give the same result twice); validity is whether the test predicts job performance; structured interviews have much higher validity than unstructured interviews

Training methods: on-the-job (rotation, apprenticeship, mentoring), off-the-job (classroom, simulations, e-learning, microlearning); effective training requires needs assessment, learning objectives, and post-training evaluation
Performance appraisal methods: graphic rating scales, BARS (behaviorally anchored rating scales), 360-degree feedback, MBO; common errors include halo effect, leniency, central tendency, recency bias

Compensation strategy: pay structure (job evaluation, market surveys), pay-for-performance (merit raises, bonuses, equity), benefits (health, retirement, time off), total rewards perspective; equity is critical (internal equity, external equity, individual equity)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Google's hiring process as documented in Laszlo Bock's Work Rules (2015). Google moved from unstructured interviews and 'brainteaser' questions (How many golf balls fit in a school bus?) to structured behavioral interviews after data showed brainteasers had zero predictive validity. Show the GCA (general cognitive ability) + structured interviews + work samples + leadership/Googleyness model and how it consistently outperforms unstructured methods. Discuss the trade-off Google made: slower hiring (5+ interviews, weeks of process) for higher quality.
setup_requirements: Sample job description and job specification
Cascio's reliability and validity coefficients chart

review.key: Hiring Is the Most Important HR Decision
review.value: Bad hires cost 1-3x annual salary in productivity loss, severance, and replacement; investing in better selection has the highest ROI of any HR activity

Decades of meta-analysis show unstructured interviews predict performance at r=0.20; structured behavioral interviews predict at r=0.50+; yet most managers continue to use unstructured interviews because they 'feel' confident in their gut judgments

Annual performance reviews bundle feedback, ranking, and compensation decisions, creating defensiveness and noise; leading firms (Adobe, Accenture, Microsoft) have replaced annual reviews with frequent check-ins

review.key: Pay Equity Is a Strategic Issue
review.value: Pay disparities by gender, race, or job class — once exposed — destroy trust and trigger lawsuits; conducting regular pay equity audits is cheaper than the alternative

learning_objectives: Compare content theories of motivation (Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland) with process theories (Vroom, Adams, Locke)
Apply expectancy theory to diagnose motivation problems in a team
Explain the limits of monetary incentives and the role of intrinsic motivation
Design a job that meets the criteria of the job characteristics model (Hackman and Oldham)
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 16 - Motivating Employees
Pink, Drive: The Surprising Truth About What Motivates Us (2009), Ch. 1-3
Hackman & Oldham, 'Motivation Through the Design of Work,' Organizational Behavior and Human Performance (1976)

lecture_notes.introduction: Motivation is the willingness to exert effort toward an organizational goal, conditioned by the effort's ability to satisfy individual needs. This definition contains the central insight of motivation theory: motivation is not a property of the employee, it is a property of the relationship between the employee and the situation. The same person who is unmotivated at one job may be deeply motivated at another. The same job may motivate one employee and bore another. Managers who say 'my team isn't motivated' are usually pointing the finger in the wrong direction. The theories we cover this session fall into two broad camps. Content theories (Maslow, Herzberg, McClelland) ask 'what motivates people?' — what needs or wants drive behavior. Process theories (Vroom, Adams, Locke) ask 'how does motivation work?' — what cognitive processes connect needs to action. Both have practical value. Content theories help you think about what to offer; process theories help you think about how the mechanics work. The most important practical lesson, well-supported by research, is that money is not the universal motivator that classical management assumed. Daniel Pink's Drive synthesizes decades of research showing that for routine work, money helps; for creative or knowledge work, money beyond a baseline can actually hurt performance, while autonomy, mastery, and purpose drive sustained engagement.
key_concepts: Maslow's hierarchy of needs: physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualization; lower-order needs must be satisfied before higher-order needs become motivating; influential but weakly supported by research

Herzberg's two-factor theory: hygiene factors (salary, working conditions, supervision, policies) prevent dissatisfaction but do not motivate; motivators (achievement, recognition, work itself, responsibility, growth) produce satisfaction and motivation

McClelland's three needs: need for achievement (nAch), need for power (nPow), need for affiliation (nAff); individuals vary in their dominant need; high nAch fits individual contributor roles, high nPow fits managerial roles
Vroom's expectancy theory: motivation = expectancy (effort → performance) × instrumentality (performance → reward) × valence (value of reward); breakdown in any link kills motivation

Adams' equity theory: people compare their input/output ratio to a referent's; perceived inequity (under or over) creates tension and motivates corrective action — work less, ask for more, or change the referent
Locke's goal-setting theory: specific and difficult goals lead to higher performance than vague or easy goals; goals must be accepted, accompanied by feedback, and within the person's capability

Self-determination theory (Deci and Ryan): autonomy, competence, and relatedness are basic psychological needs; intrinsic motivation thrives when these are met; extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation

Job characteristics model (Hackman and Oldham): five core dimensions — skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback — produce three psychological states (meaningfulness, responsibility, knowledge of results) that lead to motivation, satisfaction, and performance

Reinforcement theory: behavior is shaped by its consequences; positive reinforcement (reward), negative reinforcement (remove negative), punishment (add negative), extinction (remove reward); positive reinforcement is most effective

Equity vs equality vs need: distributive justice has three principles — equity (rewards proportional to contributions), equality (everyone gets the same), need (rewards based on requirements); appropriate rule depends on the situation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Apply expectancy theory to diagnose a real motivation problem: a sales rep who has stopped trying to hit her quota. Walk through the three links: Does she believe more effort will produce more sales (expectancy)? Does she believe more sales will produce the bonus (instrumentality)? Does she value the bonus enough (valence)? In the actual case, the rep had hit her quota the previous year and was told the bonus pool was capped — instrumentality dropped to zero, killing motivation despite high effort and ability. Discuss how the manager could fix it. Then watch a 5-minute clip of Daniel Pink's TED talk on the surprising science of motivation, focusing on the candle problem experiment.
setup_requirements: Daniel Pink Drive TED talk video

review.value: For routine work, monetary incentives improve performance; for creative work, they can hurt — too much focus on the reward narrows attention and crowds out intrinsic interest
When motivation falls, expectancy theory tells you where to look: is it ability (expectancy), reward link (instrumentality), or value of reward (valence)? Each requires a different fix

review.key: Goals Work When They Are Stretched and Specific
review.value: Vague goals don't motivate; impossibly hard goals don't motivate; specific goals at the edge of ability motivate the most
Long-term motivation comes from work that is meaningful, autonomous, and provides feedback; you cannot exhort people into engagement in a job that lacks these characteristics

class_schedule.topic: Leadership: Trait, Behavioral, Contingency, and Contemporary Approaches
learning_objectives: Trace the evolution of leadership theory from trait approaches through contingency approaches to contemporary perspectives
Compare transactional and transformational leadership and identify when each is appropriate
Describe the situational leadership model and apply it to a follower's readiness level
Distinguish between leadership and management and explain why both are needed
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 17 - Being an Effective Leader
Northouse, Leadership: Theory and Practice (9th ed.), Ch. 1-2 and Ch. 9 - Transformational Leadership
Kotter, 'What Leaders Really Do,' Harvard Business Review (December 2001)

lecture_notes.introduction: Leadership is the most studied and most contested concept in management. Tens of thousands of studies, hundreds of books, and dozens of theories have tried to identify what makes a leader effective. The field has progressed from believing leaders are born (trait theories), to believing they can be trained (behavioral theories), to believing the right leader depends on the situation (contingency theories), to focusing on the leader's ability to inspire transformation (transformational and authentic leadership). Each wave has added something useful, but no theory has produced a complete, predictive model. The honest answer is that leadership is not a single thing — it is a family of related capabilities that work in different combinations depending on the situation. This session walks through the major theoretical traditions and highlights what each contributes. We also draw the distinction between leadership and management that John Kotter made famous in the 1990s. Management is about coping with complexity through planning, organizing, and controlling. Leadership is about coping with change through setting direction, aligning people, and motivating. Both are necessary in any organization. A pure manager runs a stable shop into obsolescence; a pure leader inspires dramatic changes that may or may not work. The best executives do both, in proportions that fit the situation.
key_concepts: Trait theories: search for distinguishing characteristics that separate leaders from non-leaders; modern research finds five reliable predictors — extroversion, conscientiousness, openness to experience, emotional stability, and intelligence

Behavioral theories: focus on what leaders do, not who they are; Ohio State studies identified initiating structure (task-oriented) and consideration (people-oriented); University of Michigan studies identified production-oriented and employee-oriented leaders
Managerial Grid (Blake and Mouton): plots concern for production against concern for people; the (9,9) team management style is theoretically ideal, with high concern for both

Fiedler's contingency model: effectiveness depends on the match between leader's style (task-oriented or relationship-oriented as measured by the Least Preferred Co-worker scale) and the favorability of the situation (leader-member relations, task structure, position power)

Hersey-Blanchard situational leadership: leader behavior should adapt to follower readiness (ability and willingness); four styles — telling (low readiness), selling, participating, delegating (high readiness)

Path-goal theory (House): leader's job is to clarify the path to goals and remove obstacles; styles include directive, supportive, participative, achievement-oriented; the right style depends on subordinate and environmental characteristics

Leader-member exchange (LMX) theory: leaders develop differentiated relationships with subordinates; in-group members get more attention, resources, and rewards than out-group members, affecting performance and turnover

Transactional leadership: motivate followers through exchange — clarify role expectations, provide rewards for performance, use management-by-exception to correct deviations; effective for stable conditions and routine work

Transformational leadership: inspire followers to transcend self-interest for the good of the organization; charisma, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, individualized consideration; effective for change and innovation
Authentic leadership: leaders who know themselves, act consistently with their values, build trust through transparency; foundation for ethical leadership
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compare two real leaders and the styles they employed: Steve Jobs (charismatic, transformational, sometimes brutal — drove Apple to enormous innovation but with high turnover and conflict) and Tim Cook (operational, transactional, supply-chain focused — steady scaling and execution but criticized for less innovation). Discuss how each fit Apple's situation at the time: Jobs needed to revive a dying company, Cook needed to scale a global juggernaut. Then walk through the Hersey-Blanchard model with a concrete example: a new graduate joining a team needs telling style (high directive, low supportive); after six months they need selling (high directive, high supportive); after a year, participating; after two years on a familiar task, delegating.

review.value: Management copes with complexity (planning, budgeting, organizing, controlling); leadership copes with change (direction, alignment, motivation); both are needed and neither substitutes for the other
There is no universally best leadership style; the best leaders read the situation — task characteristics, follower readiness, environmental conditions — and adapt
When organizations need to transform, transactional leadership is not enough; transformational leaders inspire followers to do more than they thought possible
Without trust, no leadership style works; trust is built through competence, consistency, and integrity, and destroyed through one act of betrayal

learning_objectives: Describe the communication process and identify common barriers to effective communication
Distinguish between formal and informal communication channels and explain the role of the grapevine
Apply active listening and feedback techniques to improve interpersonal communication
Evaluate the effectiveness of different communication channels for different message types
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 14 - Managers and Communication
Stone, Patton & Heen, Difficult Conversations (10th anniversary ed., 2010), Ch. 1-3
Goleman, 'What Makes a Leader?' Harvard Business Review (1998, reprinted 2004)

lecture_notes.introduction: Communication is the transfer and understanding of meaning. Note both halves of that definition: transfer alone is not enough; the message must be understood as the sender intended. Estimates vary, but managers spend 60-80% of their time communicating — meetings, emails, calls, presentations, hallway conversations. The quality of those communications largely determines the manager's effectiveness. A correct decision badly communicated is often worse than a mediocre decision well communicated, because execution depends on shared understanding. Yet managers rarely receive formal training in communication beyond writing class. They learn by doing, and they pick up bad habits along with good ones. This session focuses on the practical mechanics: how communication breaks down, how to choose the right channel for the message, how to listen actively, how to give feedback that improves performance instead of triggering defensiveness, and how to navigate difficult conversations. We also cover the political reality of organizational communication — informal networks, the grapevine, and the role of nonverbal cues. The grapevine is often more accurate than the formal channels and always faster; managers who try to suppress it lose information; managers who tap into it gain a sensor network for their organization.
key_concepts: Communication process: sender encodes message, transmits through channel, receiver decodes message, provides feedback; noise can distort the message at any stage
Filtering: deliberate manipulation of information to make it more favorable to the receiver; common in upward communication where bad news gets softened
Selective perception: receivers see and hear based on their needs, motivations, experiences, and other personal characteristics; same message produces different interpretations
Information overload: more information than the receiver can process; leads to selection, filtering, and errors; modern email/Slack culture has made this worse
Nonverbal communication: body language, facial expressions, voice tone, physical distance; often contradicts verbal message; receivers tend to trust nonverbal cues more when there is conflict

Channel richness: face-to-face is highest (multiple cues, immediate feedback, personal); video, telephone, written letters, emails, formal reports, bulletins — decreasing richness; rich channels for ambiguous messages, lean for routine
Active listening: full attention to speaker, parallel-processing what is being said, demonstrating engagement, reserving judgment, asking clarifying questions, paraphrasing for understanding
Feedback (giving): specific (about behavior, not personality), timely (close to event), goal-oriented (helps recipient improve), descriptive (not evaluative), focused on what receiver can change

Difficult conversations: conversations involving conflict, emotion, or high stakes; the Stone/Patton/Heen framework distinguishes the 'what happened' conversation, the feelings conversation, and the identity conversation

Cross-cultural communication: high-context cultures (Japan, China, Saudi Arabia) rely on shared understanding and nonverbal cues; low-context cultures (US, Germany, Scandinavia) rely on explicit verbal messages; misunderstanding common in cross-cultural negotiation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play a difficult conversation: a manager needs to tell a high-performing engineer that her aggressive feedback in code reviews is alienating teammates and that she needs to change. Walk through three versions: (1) the avoidance approach — vague, indirect, no clear ask, no improvement; (2) the brutal approach — blames the person, triggers defensiveness, damages the relationship; (3) the difficult-conversations approach — acknowledges contributions, separates behavior from identity, gives specific examples, asks for the engineer's perspective, agrees on a concrete change. Discuss why version 3 produces lasting behavior change.

review.value: Use rich channels (face-to-face, video) for ambiguous, emotional, or important messages; use lean channels (email, memos) for routine, factual messages; sending a layoff notice by Slack is a known leadership failure

review.key: Active Listening Is Rare and Powerful
review.value: Most managers listen to respond, not to understand; learning to actually listen — paraphrasing, asking questions, withholding judgment — improves both decisions and relationships

review.key: Feedback Should Be Specific and Frequent
review.value: Vague annual reviews don't work; specific, timely feedback close to the event drives behavior change; the rule of thumb is 6 positive observations to every 1 corrective

review.key: The Grapevine Is a Feature, Not a Bug
review.value: Informal networks carry information faster and more reliably than formal channels; managers who try to suppress them lose visibility; managers who tap into them gain insight

learning_objectives: Distinguish between groups and teams and identify the conditions under which teams outperform groups
Describe Tuckman's stages of group development and explain how leaders can accelerate maturation
Identify the characteristics of high-performing teams from research by Hackman, Lencioni, and Google's Aristotle Project
Diagnose and address common team dysfunctions including social loafing, groupthink, and conflict mismanagement
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 13 - Managing Teams
Lencioni, The Five Dysfunctions of a Team (2002), Ch. 1-5
Duhigg, 'What Google Learned From Its Quest to Build the Perfect Team,' New York Times Magazine (February 25, 2016)

lecture_notes.introduction: Teams are everywhere in modern organizations because the work has changed. When work consisted of independent tasks performed by individual specialists, individual jobs and reporting lines were enough. As work became more interdependent, requiring multiple specialties to coordinate around a common output, teams became the natural unit of work. Today, software is built by teams, surgeries performed by teams, products designed by teams, deals closed by teams. Team effectiveness has become one of the highest-leverage management problems. The good news is that we know a lot about what makes teams work. Google's Project Aristotle, a multi-year study of 180 teams inside the company, found that the most important factor in team effectiveness was not who was on the team but how the team interacted. Specifically, psychological safety — the belief that team members can take risks without being punished or humiliated — predicted team performance better than individual talent, experience, or workload. Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions framework, developed from consulting practice rather than academic research, ranks the foundational dysfunction as absence of trust, with the other four dysfunctions building on top of it. Both perspectives converge on the same insight: team performance is largely about the social conditions the team creates, not the raw talent of its members.
key_concepts: Group vs team: a group is two or more interacting individuals who come together to achieve specific objectives; a team is a group whose members work intensely on a specific common goal using positive synergy and mutual accountability

Types of teams: functional teams (same department), problem-solving teams (improve processes), self-managed work teams (operate without manager), cross-functional teams (different specialties), virtual teams (geographically dispersed), global teams (across cultures and time zones)

Tuckman's stages: forming (orientation, dependence on leader), storming (conflict, jockeying for position), norming (cohesion, agreement on roles), performing (high productivity, low conflict), adjourning (closure, dispersion)

Effective team characteristics (Hackman): real team (clear boundaries), compelling direction (clear, challenging, consequential goal), enabling structure (right people, well-designed task, clear norms), supportive context (information, training, rewards), expert coaching (process help)
Lencioni's five dysfunctions (pyramid, bottom up): absence of trust, fear of conflict, lack of commitment, avoidance of accountability, inattention to results

Psychological safety (Edmondson): shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking; team members can speak up with ideas, questions, concerns, mistakes without fear of embarrassment or retribution
Social loafing: tendency for individuals to expend less effort in a group than alone; mitigated by individual accountability, smaller groups, meaningful tasks, and social cohesion

Groupthink: pressure for conformity restrains independent thinking; symptoms include illusion of invulnerability, collective rationalization, stereotyping outsiders, self-censorship, illusion of unanimity

Functional vs dysfunctional conflict: functional conflict supports goals, improves performance (cognitive conflict over ideas); dysfunctional conflict hinders goals, damages relationships (affective conflict over people)

Diversity in teams: cognitive diversity (different perspectives, knowledge) generally improves team performance for complex tasks; demographic diversity has more mixed effects but is a fairness issue independent of performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through Google's Project Aristotle methodology and findings. Google studied 180 teams, controlling for individual talent, team composition, and workload, looking for what made some teams systematically outperform others. The five factors that predicted team success, in order: (1) psychological safety, (2) dependability, (3) structure and clarity, (4) meaning, (5) impact. Discuss why psychological safety came first — it is the precondition for all the other behaviors that produce team performance. Then run a 5-minute exercise: assess your own current team or group on each of the five factors and identify which is the weakest.

review.key: How a Team Interacts Beats Who Is On It
review.value: Google's research shows that team norms predict performance more than the average talent of team members; a team of A players with weak norms can be outperformed by a team of B players with strong norms

Without the freedom to speak up, ask questions, and admit mistakes, all the other team-effectiveness factors break down; building psychological safety is the highest-leverage intervention for team improvement
Teams that avoid all conflict produce mediocre decisions through groupthink; the goal is functional conflict over ideas, not the absence of conflict
Calling a group a team doesn't make it one; teams form around interdependent tasks and shared accountability for results, not around shared meeting rooms

class_schedule.topic: Foundations of Control and Performance Management
learning_objectives: Describe the three steps of the control process: measuring performance, comparing to standard, taking action
Distinguish among feedforward, concurrent, and feedback control
Apply the balanced scorecard to monitor performance across financial, customer, internal, and learning perspectives
Explain the dysfunctional consequences of poorly designed control systems
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), Ch. 18 - Monitoring and Controlling
Kaplan & Norton, 'The Balanced Scorecard — Measures That Drive Performance,' Harvard Business Review (1992, reprinted 2005)
Simons, Levers of Control (1995), Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Control is the process of monitoring activities to ensure they are being accomplished as planned and correcting any significant deviations. Without control, plans are wishes. With excessive or wrong-headed control, plans become bureaucratic exercises that distort behavior in damaging ways. Designing the right control system is one of the hardest jobs in management because what gets measured gets managed — and often manipulated. Sears once incentivized auto repair shops on revenue per visit. The result was massive overcharging and a regulatory scandal that cost the company hundreds of millions and damaged its brand for decades. Wells Fargo incentivized branch staff on cross-selling and ended up with employees opening millions of fake accounts to hit their numbers. Both control systems delivered exactly what they were designed to incentivize. The lesson is that control system design is not a clerical activity. It is a core management responsibility with strategic and ethical implications. Robert Kaplan and David Norton's Balanced Scorecard, introduced in 1992, was an attempt to fix the most common control failure — over-reliance on financial measures that report past performance and miss the leading indicators of future performance. The scorecard adds three non-financial perspectives — customer, internal process, and learning and growth — to create a more complete picture. We also examine Robert Simons' framework for understanding control systems as 'levers' that managers can pull in different combinations depending on the situation.
key_concepts: The control process: measure actual performance, compare against a standard, take managerial action (correct the deviation, revise the standard, or take no action)

Three types of control by timing: feedforward control (anticipates problems before they occur — quality input checking, preventive maintenance), concurrent control (occurs while activity is in progress — direct supervision, real-time dashboards), feedback control (after activity is completed — financial reports, customer satisfaction surveys)
Sources of measurement: personal observation, statistical reports, oral reports, written reports; each has different cost, timeliness, and richness

Range of variation: acceptable parameters of variance between actual and standard; only deviations outside this range trigger action; setting the range too narrow creates noise, too wide misses real problems

Bureaucratic vs decentralized control: bureaucratic control relies on formal rules, regulations, and authority; decentralized control relies on shared values, group norms, and trust; technology and knowledge work have shifted toward decentralized control

Balanced scorecard perspectives: financial (How do we look to shareholders?), customer (How do customers see us?), internal process (What must we excel at?), learning and growth (Can we continue to improve and create value?)

Lead vs lag indicators: lag indicators report past performance (revenue, profit, customer churn); lead indicators predict future performance (employee engagement, customer satisfaction, pipeline value)

Simons' four levers of control: belief systems (mission, values), boundary systems (limits, rules), diagnostic control systems (monitoring against goals), interactive control systems (used by senior managers to drive learning)

Dysfunctional control consequences: behavior displacement (focusing on measured activities at expense of unmeasured), gaming the metrics (manipulating data without changing performance), measurement-driven myopia (short-term thinking), sub-optimization (local optimization that hurts the whole)
Goodhart's Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure; the act of incentivizing the measure changes behavior in ways that distort the measure
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through the Wells Fargo cross-selling scandal as a case of catastrophic control system failure. Wells set a goal of 'eight is great' — eight financial products per customer — and tied compensation aggressively to cross-selling. Over years, employees facing impossible quotas opened millions of unauthorized accounts, signed customers up for products without consent, and created fake email addresses. The control system measured and rewarded the wrong thing (account openings) instead of what mattered (customer well-being and retention).
