The result: $3 billion in fines, criminal charges, the destruction of the brand, and the firing of multiple CEOs. Discuss how a balanced scorecard with leading indicators of customer satisfaction and complaint volume could have caught the problem years earlier.

review.value: And what gets managed gets gamed if the incentives are strong enough; control system designers must consider how the system will be manipulated, not just how it will work in honest hands

review.key: Financial Measures Alone Are Not Enough
review.value: Financial measures are lagging indicators; by the time they show problems, the underlying causes have been festering for quarters; balanced scorecards add leading indicators that catch problems earlier
Routine work tolerates bureaucratic control; knowledge work requires decentralized control because the manager cannot directly supervise the cognitive process
Any measure that becomes a target will be optimized, sometimes by changing the measured behavior, sometimes by gaming the data; rotate measures, use multiple measures, and watch for distortion

class_schedule.topic: Course Synthesis: The Manager's Toolkit and Continuing Development
learning_objectives: Synthesize the four management functions into an integrated model of managerial work
Identify the most important transitions managers face in their careers and the skills each requires
Develop a personal management development plan based on self-assessment and feedback
Evaluate emerging trends including AI in management, remote work, and sustainability
readings: Robbins & Coulter, Management (15th ed.), epilogue and selected end-of-chapter cases
Watkins, The First 90 Days (updated ed., 2013), Ch. 1-3
Drucker, 'Managing Oneself,' Harvard Business Review (1999, reprinted 2005)

lecture_notes.introduction: We have covered fourteen weeks of management theory, frameworks, and cases. This final session pulls the threads together and looks forward. The first task is synthesis: how do the four functions actually interconnect in the work of a manager, and what does it look like to integrate them rather than treat them as separate compartments? The second task is self-assessment: where do you stand on the skills the course has emphasized, and what should you do next to develop them? The third task is looking forward: what trends are reshaping management in the 2020s and 2030s, and how will the toolkit you have learned need to adapt? The most important career transition for any manager is the move from individual contributor to first-line manager. It is widely cited as the hardest single transition in a working life. The skills that made you successful as an individual — technical excellence, personal productivity, working independently — are not the skills that make you successful as a manager. In fact, managers who hang onto their individual contributor identity often fail. Watkins' First 90 Days is the canonical guide to this transition: do not try to prove yourself by doing the work you used to do; spend the first ninety days listening, learning the new role, and building relationships with your team and stakeholders. Drucker's 'Managing Oneself' essay is the canonical guide to the longer game: know your strengths, know how you perform best, know your values, know where you belong, know what to contribute. Self-knowledge is the foundation of every other management skill.
key_concepts: Integration of POLC: in practice, planning informs organizing decisions; organizing creates structures that enable leading; leading shapes what gets controlled; controlling produces feedback that updates plans — a continuous cycle, not a linear sequence

Manager development phases: technical competence → first-line management (people skills) → middle management (functional integration) → general management (cross-functional and strategic) → senior leadership (organizational and external)
Transition shocks: each promotion is a different job, not a higher-level version of the previous job; failing to redefine the job is the most common reason for derailment
Personal SWOT: an individual's strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats; the self-assessment foundation for development planning
Drucker's questions for self-management: What are my strengths? How do I perform? What are my values? Where do I belong? What can I contribute?

AI and the future of management: routine analytical tasks (forecasting, scheduling, performance scoring) are being automated; management work shifts toward judgment, ethics, coaching, and the design of human-AI collaboration
Hybrid and remote work: post-pandemic norm in many industries; requires new skills in asynchronous communication, written-first culture, deliberate inclusion, and trust-based control

Sustainability and stakeholder capitalism: ESG pressures from investors, employees, regulators, and customers; managers must balance multiple objectives rather than optimize for shareholder return alone
Evidence-based management: applying the best available research evidence to management decisions, similar to evidence-based medicine; reduces reliance on fads and personal experience

Continuous learning: management is not a degree you earn once; the field changes, the situations change, and what worked yesterday may fail tomorrow; the best managers are lifelong students of the discipline
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each student conducts a personal management SWOT and shares one strength they will leverage and one weakness they will work on this year. Then walk through the Watkins First 90 Days framework as preparation for graduates' first management role: organize your learning (technical, cultural, political), match strategy to situation (start-up, turnaround, accelerated growth, realignment, sustaining success), secure early wins, negotiate success with your boss, achieve alignment, build your team, create coalitions. End the course by returning to Mintzberg's empirical reality of managerial work and asking each student: now that you know what management really looks like, do you still want the job? The honest answers are interesting.

review.key: Management Is a Discipline, Not a Talent
review.value: It can be learned, practiced, and improved; the skills covered in this course are starting points, not endpoints; expect to be still learning twenty years into your career

review.key: The Hardest Move Is From IC to Manager
review.value: The skills that got you promoted will not make you a successful manager; expect to feel incompetent for the first six months, and resist the urge to retreat into individual contributor work
Knowing your strengths, values, and how you work best is more important than mastering any specific framework; Drucker's 'Managing Oneself' is the most underrated essay in the management canon

AI, remote work, stakeholder capitalism, and demographic shifts are reshaping management; the toolkit you have learned will need to be updated continuously; treat this course as a foundation, not a destination

quizzes.topic: Managerial Roles, History, and the External Environment

questions.question: A new CEO of a regional grocery chain spends her first week as follows: Monday morning attending a community ribbon-cutting (figurehead), Monday afternoon meeting with a major supplier (negotiator and liaison), Tuesday reviewing weekly sales reports and flagging anomalies (monitor), Wednesday hosting an analyst day for investors (spokesperson), Thursday in budget meetings reallocating capex (resource allocator), Friday handling a viral customer service complaint on social media (disturbance handler). (a) Map each activity to one or more of Mintzberg's ten managerial roles. (b) Which of the three Mintzberg categories — interpersonal, informational, or decisional — dominated her week? (c) How does this empirical pattern compare to the classical POLC view of management?
questions.answer: (a) Mapping: Monday AM ribbon-cutting = figurehead (interpersonal); Monday PM supplier meeting = negotiator (decisional) and liaison (interpersonal); Tuesday sales report review = monitor (informational); Wednesday analyst day = spokesperson (informational); Thursday budget reallocation = resource allocator (decisional); Friday customer crisis = disturbance handler (decisional). (b) The week is roughly 3 decisional, 2 informational, 2 interpersonal — decisional roles dominate, consistent with Mintzberg's findings that decisional roles are the most distinctive managerial work. (c) The classical POLC view depicts managers as planning, organizing, leading, and controlling in deliberate sequence. The empirical pattern is fragmented, reactive, and interrupt-driven. Mintzberg's research showed that managers spend most time in brief activities responding to immediate demands rather than in solitary contemplation. The CEO's week reflects this reality: every day she shifts roles in response to external events, with little time for the calm POLC cycle.

rubric.key: Correct mapping of all six activities to Mintzberg roles
Correct identification of dominant category with reasoning
Coherent comparison to classical POLC view

questions.question: Compare Frederick Taylor's scientific management with Elton Mayo's human relations approach. For each, identify (a) the central insight, (b) one modern management practice that descends from it, and (c) one criticism of taking the approach to its extreme.
questions.answer: Taylor — scientific management. (a) Central insight: work can be analyzed scientifically to find one best method, raising productivity and wages simultaneously. (b) Modern descendant: industrial engineering and time-and-motion study used in fast food job design (e.g., McDonald's prep times) and Amazon warehouse picking. (c) Criticism: ignores worker autonomy and dignity; reduces workers to interchangeable parts; can produce monotony, injury, and resistance. Mayo — human relations. (a) Central insight: worker productivity depends on social and psychological factors, not just economic incentives; the act of being observed and treated as important raises engagement (Hawthorne Effect). (b) Modern descendant: employee engagement surveys, recognition programs, manager training in interpersonal skills, all of Google's People Operations. (c) Criticism: can become paternalistic; risks substituting feel-good interventions for real structural changes (pay, conditions, autonomy); empirical replication of Hawthorne Studies has been mixed.

rubric.key: Taylor: correct insight, descendant, and criticism
Mayo: correct insight, descendant, and criticism

questions.question: Apply Porter's Five Forces to the U.S. airline industry as it stood after consolidation through the 2010s (American/US Airways, Delta/Northwest, United/Continental, Southwest as the largest carriers). Identify whether each force is favorable or unfavorable to airline profitability, and conclude with an overall assessment of industry attractiveness.
questions.answer: Threat of new entrants: LOW/FAVORABLE — capital intensity (aircraft, slots, gates), regulatory barriers, hub network effects, brand loyalty programs all deter entry; recent attempts (Avelo, Breeze) struggle to scale. Bargaining power of suppliers: HIGH/UNFAVORABLE — Boeing and Airbus are a duopoly; pilots are unionized with strong leverage; airports and air traffic control are monopolies; jet fuel prices set globally. Bargaining power of buyers: MIXED — leisure travelers have high price sensitivity and switching is easy via OTAs (Expedia, Kayak), giving them power; business travelers (and the loyalty programs that lock them in) are less price sensitive; corporate contracts provide some predictability. Threat of substitutes: MODERATE — for short routes, driving and rail compete; for long routes, no real substitute; videoconferencing reduced some business travel post-2020. Rivalry: HIGH/UNFAVORABLE despite consolidation — perishable inventory (an empty seat at takeoff is gone forever), high fixed costs, and undifferentiated product mean carriers fight on price; consolidation has improved profitability versus the 2000s but rivalry remains intense. OVERALL: airlines collectively earn modest returns at best. The industry is structurally unattractive because powerful suppliers and intense rivalry compress margins, even though new entry is hard and substitutes are limited. Warren Buffett famously avoided airlines for decades for this reason.

rubric.key: Correct analysis of all five forces with specific evidence
Coherent overall conclusion linking forces to profitability

questions.question: An online retailer's purchasing manager discovers that switching to a Bangladeshi supplier would cut unit costs by 22% but the supplier is known to use child labor. The retailer's code of ethics prohibits child labor, but enforcement in Bangladesh is uncertain. The CEO is pressuring her to find cost savings before quarterly earnings. (a) Identify the stakeholders affected by this decision. (b) Apply each of the four ethical frameworks (utilitarian, rights, justice, integrative social contracts) to the choice. (c) What additional information should she gather before making a recommendation?
questions.answer: (a) Stakeholders: shareholders (cost savings boost earnings), customers (lower prices possible), employees of the retailer (job security tied to cost competitiveness), employees of the Bangladeshi supplier (jobs may depend on the contract — adults), child workers (harm), community in Bangladesh (economic spillovers), regulators and watchdog NGOs, the CEO and the purchasing manager personally (career and reputation). (b) Utilitarian: counts total welfare; saving 22% on costs benefits many shareholders and customers but harms a smaller number of children significantly; depends heavily on weights and time horizons. Rights: child labor violates fundamental rights of the children regardless of the aggregate benefit; rejection of the supplier is required under this framework. Justice: distributing benefits to wealthy shareholders by imposing harm on poor children fails the difference principle; deeply unjust. Integrative social contracts: local norm in some Bangladesh contexts may tolerate child work; universal hypernorms (no slavery, no child labor) override; global hypernorm wins. (c) Additional information: how confident is the supplier audit; are alternative suppliers available at intermediate cost; would the children be better or worse off if the contract were declined (sometimes they go to worse work); what is the retailer's policy on supplier remediation; is there a third-party certification that would shift accountability; what does the legal exposure look like under the UK Modern Slavery Act and similar laws.

rubric.key: Comprehensive stakeholder identification
Correct application of all four ethical frameworks
Substantive list of additional information needed

questions.question: Use expectancy theory to diagnose the following situation. A senior analyst has been a top performer for years. This year his effort and output have dropped. In a one-on-one he says: 'I'm working hard but the model I built last quarter was rejected by the new VP without explanation. Even if I produce great work, I don't think it will get used. The promotion track has been frozen anyway, so even if my work were used I wouldn't get the raise I was expecting.' Identify which links in expectancy theory are broken and propose a specific managerial action for each.
questions.answer: Expectancy theory: motivation = expectancy (effort → performance) × instrumentality (performance → reward) × valence (value of reward). Diagnosis: (1) Expectancy link is partially broken: 'my model was rejected without explanation' suggests the analyst has lost confidence that hard work will produce performance the new VP recognizes — he doesn't know what 'good' means. Action: meet with the VP and the analyst to clarify performance criteria and provide feedback on the rejected model so the analyst learns what is valued. (2) Instrumentality is broken: 'even if I produce great work, I don't think it will get used' — performance does not seem to lead to outcomes. Action: create visible feedback loops where good work is recognized and applied, perhaps a monthly review where decisions made on the basis of analyst output are highlighted. (3) Instrumentality is also broken at the financial reward link: promotion track is frozen, so good performance does not lead to the expected raise. Action: be transparent about the freeze, explain the timeline, and offer alternative recognition (project lead role, conference attendance, sabbatical) that the analyst values. Valence: the analyst clearly valued the promotion, so simply removing the reward without substitution kills motivation; substitute valued rewards.

rubric.key: Correct identification of which links are broken
Specific managerial actions matched to each broken link

questions.question: Convert the following weak goal statements into SMART goals: (a) 'Improve customer satisfaction this year.' (b) 'Reduce turnover among engineers.' (c) 'Launch a new product line.' For each, write a SMART version and explain which SMART criterion the original most clearly violated.
questions.answer: (a) SMART version: 'Increase customer satisfaction (measured by NPS) from current 32 to 45 by December 31, 2026, by reducing average support response time from 24 hours to 4 hours and adding live chat to the top 5 product pages.' Original violation: most clearly violated MEASURABLE (no metric) and TIME-BOUND (no deadline beyond 'this year'). (b) SMART version: 'Reduce voluntary annual turnover among engineers from 18% to 12% by Q4 2026 by implementing competitive equity refresh, manager training, and a structured stay-interview program.' Original violation: most clearly violated MEASURABLE (no target reduction) and SPECIFIC (no method). (c) SMART version: 'Launch the Pro tier of the analytics product to 500 paying customers generating $2M ARR by August 31, 2026, with a feature set documented in PRD-2026-03.' Original violation: most clearly violated MEASURABLE (no scale) and SPECIFIC (which product line, what does success look like).

rubric.key: Three substantive SMART rewrites
Correct identification of violated criteria

questions.question: Apply the VRIO framework to assess whether the following resources/capabilities create sustainable competitive advantage. For each, classify as: not valuable, valuable but not rare, valuable and rare but imitable, valuable rare and inimitable but not organized, or full sustainable competitive advantage. Justify each. (a) Costco's distribution network and bulk-purchasing scale. (b) Disney's library of intellectual property (Marvel, Star Wars, Pixar, classic Disney). (c) A pharmaceutical company's ISO 9001 quality certification.
questions.answer: (a) Costco's distribution and scale: Valuable (yes — drives lower unit costs that competitors cannot match), Rare (yes — only Walmart and a few global retailers operate at comparable scale), Inimitable (yes — would take a competitor decades and tens of billions to replicate the warehouse network and supplier relationships), Organized (yes — Costco's entire business model is built around exploiting scale via low markups and high volume). FULL SUSTAINABLE COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE. (b) Disney's IP library: Valuable (yes — generates billions across films, parks, merchandise, streaming), Rare (yes — no other studio holds equivalent IP), Inimitable (yes — competitors cannot create another Marvel cinematic universe with the same characters and history), Organized (yes — Disney is structured to monetize IP across multiple channels: Disney+, parks, consumer products, films). FULL SUSTAINABLE COMPETITIVE ADVANTAGE — arguably the strongest in entertainment. (c) ISO 9001 certification: Valuable (yes — required for many contracts), Rare (NO — tens of thousands of pharmaceutical firms hold ISO 9001). VALUABLE BUT NOT RARE; provides parity, not advantage. The certification is table stakes, not a moat.

rubric.key: Correct VRIO classification of Costco with reasoning
Correct VRIO classification of Disney with reasoning
Correct VRIO classification of ISO certification with reasoning

questions.question: A 50-person software startup is considering moving from a flat structure (everyone reports to the founder/CEO) to either a functional structure (engineering, sales, marketing, customer success) or a product-team structure (cross-functional squads each owning a product area). Discuss the trade-offs and recommend a structure for this stage.
questions.answer: Functional structure trade-offs: PRO — deep specialization, clear career paths within function, easier hiring (clear role definitions), efficient when work is repeatable. CON — silos, slow cross-functional coordination, decisions about a product require consensus across functions, conflict between functions over priorities. Product-team structure trade-offs: PRO — fast end-to-end ownership, single point of accountability for a product, easier to ship, better customer focus. CON — duplication of skills across teams (each squad needs an engineer, designer, PM), harder to maintain technical consistency, weaker career progression within a discipline, may not have enough headcount per squad to staff fully. RECOMMENDATION for a 50-person startup: a hybrid leaning toward product teams. At 50 people the startup is past the stage where everyone can know everything (the flat structure is failing), but too small for full functional silos (would create 4-person 'departments' that are expensive in management overhead). Form 3-5 product-focused squads each with the engineers and PM they need, but maintain functional 'chapters' or 'guilds' for engineering, design, and product to share practices and develop talent (Spotify-style). This preserves speed and end-to-end ownership while maintaining some functional cohesion. Re-evaluate at 150 people.

rubric.key: Substantive trade-off analysis of both structures
Defensible recommendation tailored to the stage

questions.question: A hiring manager wants to use unstructured interviews because she trusts her gut and has 'always picked good people.' She points to her current team as evidence. Explain (a) the empirical research on the validity of unstructured interviews, (b) the cognitive biases that make her confidence likely overstated, and (c) what selection process you would recommend instead.
questions.answer: (a) Empirical research: meta-analyses (Schmidt and Hunter 1998 and updates) show unstructured interviews have a validity coefficient of about r=0.20 — explaining roughly 4% of the variance in job performance. Structured behavioral interviews are about r=0.50, work samples r=0.54, general cognitive ability tests r=0.51. Unstructured interviews are among the WEAKEST predictors of job performance widely used in selection. (b) Cognitive biases inflating her confidence: confirmation bias (she remembers her hires who succeeded and forgets those who left or underperformed), survivorship bias (she only sees the people she chose, not the quality of the people she rejected — she may have rejected stronger candidates), illusion of skill (a small sample of decisions plus randomness produces some correct calls; she attributes success to skill rather than chance), halo effect (positive traits like attractiveness or articulate speech bleed into perceptions of competence), similarity bias (preferring candidates like herself), the Lake Wobegon effect (most people overestimate their ability at any skill, including hiring). (c) Recommended selection process: structured behavioral interviews (same questions for every candidate, scored on a rubric), a work sample relevant to the actual job (a coding exercise, a sales role-play, a writing test), a general mental ability test where legally appropriate, multiple interviewers whose scores are averaged independently before discussion, standardized note-taking. This combination has the highest validity in the selection literature and is what Google moved to after auditing its own data.

rubric.key: Accurate citation of validity coefficients
Identification of multiple cognitive biases
Specific evidence-based selection process

questions.question: A team of software engineers has high cognitive ability and strong technical skills but their output is mediocre. The team lead notices: (1) team members rarely speak up in meetings even when they disagree, (2) blame for failures is common and people avoid taking responsibility, (3) decisions are made by the senior engineer regardless of others' input. Diagnose using both Lencioni's Five Dysfunctions and Edmondson's psychological safety construct, and recommend two specific interventions.
questions.answer: Lencioni diagnosis: the team is suffering from absence of trust (the foundational dysfunction), which prevents people from being vulnerable enough to speak up, ask questions, or admit mistakes. This in turn produces fear of conflict (no real debate happens — people stay silent), lack of commitment (without debate, people don't truly buy in), avoidance of accountability (people don't hold each other to commitments they didn't make), and inattention to results (the team is not actually pursuing collective performance). All five dysfunctions are present, with absence of trust as the root cause. Edmondson diagnosis: the team has very LOW psychological safety. Members do not believe it is safe to take interpersonal risks — speaking up, asking questions, admitting errors, or challenging the senior engineer. Low safety predicts exactly the behaviors observed: silence, blame, deference. Google's Project Aristotle found psychological safety was the single strongest predictor of team performance. Recommended interventions: (1) Reframe failures as learning opportunities — the team lead publicly admits her own mistakes in retrospectives, asks questions she doesn't know the answer to in meetings, and explicitly thanks team members who raise concerns or disagree. This models psychological safety from above. Behavior change must come from leadership first; the team will not feel safe until they see that speaking up has no cost. (2) Restructure decision-making to require dissent — adopt a 'red team' or pre-mortem practice where someone is explicitly assigned the role of challenging the senior engineer's proposals. This decouples disagreement from personal conflict and makes it a job requirement. Combined with the leadership modeling, this should over months rebuild trust and unlock the team's underutilized cognitive capacity.

rubric.key: Correct Lencioni diagnosis showing root cause
Correct application of psychological safety construct
Two specific evidence-based interventions

questions.question: Compare transformational and transactional leadership. For each, give (a) a definition, (b) one famous real-world example, (c) one situation where the style fits well, and (d) one situation where the style fails.
questions.answer: Transactional leadership. (a) Definition: motivates followers through exchange — clarifying role and task expectations and providing rewards or corrections in response to performance. Components include contingent reward and management-by-exception. (b) Example: most plant managers and military officers in routine peacetime operations; less famous because transactional leadership rarely makes magazine covers. Tim Cook is closer to transactional than transformational — his strength is operational excellence and supplier management, not vision-casting. (c) Fits: stable environments with clear performance metrics, routine work, employees who value clarity and predictability, military and emergency operations where command-and-control is required. (d) Fails: rapidly changing environments requiring innovation, knowledge work where motivation comes from purpose, organizations needing to change strategy or culture. Transformational leadership. (a) Definition: inspires followers to transcend self-interest for the good of the organization through charisma, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, and individualized consideration. (b) Example: Steve Jobs at Apple, Satya Nadella's transformation of Microsoft from Ballmer-era stagnation to growth, Mary Barra's culture change at GM, Anne Mulcahy at Xerox. (c) Fits: organizations in crisis or needing transformation, knowledge work, creative work, when followers are seeking meaning beyond paycheck, scaling startups. (d) Fails: when followers want stability and predictability, when the leader's vision is wrong (charisma applied to a bad strategy can be devastating), when the work is routine and execution-focused.

rubric.key: Correct definition of both styles
Real-world examples for both
Situations where each fits and fails

questions.question: A manager needs to deliver bad news: a beloved long-tenured employee is being laid off due to a department restructuring. The employee is not at fault and has been a strong performer. (a) What channel should the manager use, and why? (b) What should the manager say in the first 60 seconds of the conversation? (c) What two mistakes should she avoid?
questions.answer: (a) Channel: face-to-face, in private, preferably in person if at all possible; if remote, video call with cameras on. Channel richness research is unambiguous: emotional, ambiguous, and high-stakes messages require the richest possible channel. Email or Slack for a layoff is a textbook leadership failure (see Vishal Garg, Better.com, December 2021 — 900 employees laid off via Zoom meeting and the CEO's reputation never recovered). The face-to-face channel allows the manager to convey care through nonverbal cues, answer questions immediately, and adjust to the employee's emotional response. (b) First 60 seconds: 'Thank you for coming. I need to share difficult news with you. The company is restructuring our department, and your position is being eliminated. This is not about your performance — you have been a valued employee and a strong contributor. I want to walk you through what this means and the support we will provide. I know this is hard. Take a moment if you need it.' Key elements: lead with the news (don't drag it out with small talk), be direct (no euphemisms), explicitly decouple from performance, signal care and support, give space for the emotional response. (c) Two mistakes to avoid: (1) Filtering or softening to the point of confusion — saying 'we're rethinking some roles' instead of 'your position is being eliminated' leaves the employee unsure what was just said and prolongs the pain. Be clear. (2) Defending the company or blaming others — 'I fought for you but they wouldn't listen' or 'this is corporate's decision, not mine' shifts focus from the employee's situation to the manager's discomfort and undermines the company's credibility. Own the message, even if you didn't make the decision.

rubric.key: Correct channel choice with rich-channel reasoning
Substantive first-60-seconds script
Two specific mistakes to avoid

questions.question: A retail bank designs a new control system for its branches: each branch is measured weekly on (1) number of new accounts opened, (2) number of products sold per customer, (3) revenue per teller hour. Compensation for branch managers and tellers is heavily tied to these metrics. (a) Predict the unintended consequences using Goodhart's Law and the Wells Fargo case as a reference. (b) Propose a revised control system using balanced scorecard principles that would catch these problems earlier. (c) What leading indicators would you add?
questions.answer: (a) Predicted unintended consequences: All three metrics are highly gameable, especially under heavy compensation pressure. New accounts opened: tellers will open accounts customers did not authorize (Wells Fargo's exact failure mode — millions of fake accounts). Products per customer: tellers will push customers into products they don't need (overdraft protection, credit cards, insurance) to hit the cross-sell quota. Revenue per teller hour: tellers will rush customers, decline complex transactions, refuse to help with anything that doesn't generate revenue, degrading customer experience and trust. All three metrics measure activity rather than customer well-being or branch health, and Goodhart's Law applies — they will be optimized by behavior that destroys the underlying value. The Wells Fargo case showed exactly this trajectory: cross-sell metric, aggressive compensation, fake accounts, billions in fines, criminal charges, and reputational damage that took years to heal. (b) Revised control system using balanced scorecard: FINANCIAL — revenue per branch, profit margin, cost-to-income ratio. CUSTOMER — Net Promoter Score, customer retention rate, complaint volume per 1000 customers, share of wallet (a longer-term outcome metric). INTERNAL PROCESS — customer wait time, error rate in transactions, audit findings. LEARNING AND GROWTH — employee engagement, voluntary turnover, training completion rates. The balanced scorecard forces a multi-dimensional view that activity metrics alone cannot provide. (c) Leading indicators to add: customer complaint volume (rising complaints predict future churn and regulatory action), employee anonymous concern reports (a culture survey question like 'I am asked to do things I disagree with' would have flagged Wells Fargo years before the scandal), product cancellation rate within 30 days of opening (a high cancellation rate suggests products were not wanted), mystery shopper results assessing whether sales pressure feels appropriate. These leading indicators turn the slow lag of financial damage into a fast-feedback warning system.

rubric.key: Correct prediction of unintended consequences with Goodhart's Law
Revised balanced scorecard covering all four perspectives
Specific leading indicators with reasoning

questions.question: A software engineer has just been promoted to first-line manager of a team of seven engineers, several of whom were her peers a week ago. Using Watkins' First 90 Days framework and Drucker's 'Managing Oneself' insights, recommend her priorities for the first month.
questions.answer: Watkins' First 90 Days priorities adapted to this transition: WEEK 1 — Listen, do not act. Resist the strong urge to prove herself by writing code or shipping features. Her job has changed, and the skills that made her a top engineer are not the skills that will make her a successful manager. WEEK 1-2 — One-on-one meetings with each direct report. Ask: what are you working on, what are your career goals, what could the previous manager have done better, what should I know about how this team works, what are the unwritten rules. Listen more than talk. WEEK 2-3 — Meet with stakeholders outside the team: the manager's own manager, peer managers, key customers of the team's output (product, design). Understand what success looks like from each perspective. WEEK 3-4 — Diagnose the situation type (Watkins' STARS framework: Start-up, Turnaround, Accelerated growth, Realignment, Sustaining success). Each calls for different strategies. WEEK 3-4 — Identify 2-3 early wins that demonstrate leadership without being premature reorganizations. Address one or two known pain points (broken on-call, ambiguous priorities, unclear coding standards). Drucker's insights to apply: KNOW HOW YOU PERFORM — the new manager should reflect on whether she works best as a reader or listener, in mornings or afternoons, in big projects or many small ones — and structure her management routines accordingly. KNOW YOUR VALUES — what matters to her, and where will she draw lines (e.g., is she willing to push the team to weekend work). KNOW WHAT TO CONTRIBUTE — what unique value can she add as a manager beyond what the previous manager did. The hardest part: stop coding. Her role is now to make her team more effective, not to write the most code herself. The transition from individual contributor to manager is the hardest career transition because it requires letting go of the identity that earned the promotion.

rubric.key: Correct application of First 90 Days framework
Substantive use of Drucker insights
Identification of the IC-to-manager transition challenge

questions.question: Over the past 14 weeks you have studied four management functions, dozens of theories, and many cases. In one paragraph, identify the single most important insight from this course that you will carry into your career, and explain why. (Open-ended; full credit for thoughtful, specific answers grounded in the course material.)
questions.answer: Sample full-credit answer: The single most important insight from this course is that management is fundamentally about trade-offs that cannot be eliminated, only chosen. Every framework we studied — generic strategies, mechanistic vs organic structures, transactional vs transformational leadership, financial vs balanced scorecards — exposed a tension between values that pull in opposite directions: speed vs consistency, autonomy vs coordination, innovation vs efficiency, short-term results vs long-term capability. The temptation, both in textbooks and in real organizations, is to pretend you can have both at no cost. The reality is that every choice excludes other choices, and the manager's job is to make those choices consciously rather than letting them happen by default. This insight changes how I will approach problems: instead of looking for the right answer, I will look for the trade-off being made, ask whether it is the right trade-off for this situation, and accept that the cost is real. The maturity of a manager is measured by how clearly they see their trade-offs and how honestly they own them. (Other defensible answers: psychological safety as foundation of team performance; the IC-to-manager transition; Goodhart's Law; the difference between leadership and management; the value of explicit goals; the importance of self-knowledge.)

rubric.key: Specific insight grounded in course material
Coherent reasoning about why it matters

major_assignments.title: Company Diagnostic: Environment, Strategy, and Structure
major_assignments.description: Choose a publicly traded company (not Apple, Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Tesla, or any company analyzed in class). Conduct a comprehensive diagnostic of its external environment, internal capabilities, strategic position, and organizational structure. Your goal is to produce the kind of one-shot company analysis that a management consultant or buy-side equity analyst would deliver. Synthesize publicly available information from annual reports (10-K), proxy statements, earnings calls, analyst reports, and industry publications.
objectives: Apply PESTEL and Porter's Five Forces to analyze the external environment systematically
Apply VRIO to identify sources of competitive advantage (or its absence)
Classify the company's strategic position using Porter's generic strategies
Diagnose the organizational structure and assess fit with strategy
Make a substantiated recommendation to the company's leadership
requirements: PESTEL analysis with at least 3 specific factors per category, citing sources
Porter's Five Forces analysis with quantitative or qualitative evidence for each force
VRIO analysis of at least 3 key resources or capabilities
Strategic position statement with classification under Porter's generics
One concrete recommendation with implementation considerations
Bibliography with at least 10 sources, including the most recent 10-K and proxy statement
deliverables: Source list with verified URLs or citations
Optional appendix with financial trend exhibits
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on depth of analysis (use of frameworks accurately and substantively), quality of evidence (specific facts, not generalities), coherence (the analysis tells a consistent story), and actionability of the recommendation (specific, defensible, addresses a real issue).

rubric.key: PESTEL analysis with specific evidence
Five Forces analysis with reasoning
VRIO analysis of resources/capabilities
Strategic position classification with justification
Structure diagnosis and fit assessment
Specific defensible recommendation
Quality of writing, citations, and exhibits

major_assignments.title: Motivation and Leadership Case Analysis
major_assignments.description: Read the Harvard Business School case 'Erik Peterson at Biometra (A) and (B)' (or an equivalent assigned by the instructor — alternatives include 'David Fletcher,' 'The Young Manager,' or 'Heidi Roizen'). Analyze the case from multiple management perspectives: motivation theory, leadership theory, communication, and team dynamics. Then write a recommendation memo addressed to the protagonist as if you were a trusted mentor.
objectives: Apply at least three motivation theories (e.g., expectancy, equity, job characteristics) to diagnose the situation
Identify the leadership style being used and propose a more effective alternative
Recommend specific communication and team-building actions
Write a clear, actionable memo that the protagonist could realistically use
requirements: Read the assigned case thoroughly (HBS case, ~10-20 pages)
Memo format: TO/FROM/DATE/SUBJECT, executive summary, analysis, recommendations, expected outcomes
Reference at least 3 distinct theories from the course
Cite at least 2 academic sources beyond the textbook
deliverables: Reference list of theories and academic sources used
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on diagnostic accuracy (correct identification of root issues), theoretical depth (multiple frameworks applied non-superficially), specificity of recommendations (the protagonist could actually do these), and quality of writing.

rubric.key: Multi-theory motivation diagnosis
Leadership analysis with specific style identification
Communication and team-building recommendations
Realistic implementation considerations
Quality of memo writing and structure

major_assignments.title: Personal Management Development Plan
major_assignments.description: Reflect on your strengths, weaknesses, values, and career aspirations using the frameworks and self-assessment tools introduced in this course. Produce a personal management development plan that you could realistically follow over the next 24 months. The plan should be honest, specific, and actionable — not aspirational fluff.
objectives: Conduct a personal SWOT analysis based on multiple sources of feedback
Apply Drucker's 'Managing Oneself' questions to your own situation
Identify specific management skills to develop and how
Set 3-5 SMART goals for the next 24 months
Identify role models, mentors, and resources to support development
requirements: Reflection on Drucker's five questions (two pages)
Skill gap analysis: top 3 skills to develop and concrete actions for each (two pages)
List of resources: books, courses, mentors, role models
Optional: peer or supervisor feedback collected for input (anonymous okay)
deliverables: Written plan submitted as PDF (8-12 pages)
major_assignments.grading_rubric: Graded on honesty (real weaknesses identified, not vanity weaknesses), specificity (concrete actions, not vague intentions), framework grounding (uses course concepts appropriately), and realism (could actually be executed).

rubric.key: Honest, evidence-based SWOT
Substantive engagement with Drucker's questions
Specific skill development plan with actions
SMART goal quality and realism
Coherent identification of resources and supports

weekly_quizzes.description: Five quizzes (weeks 3, 6, 9, 12, 15) covering history, environment, ethics, decision-making, planning, strategy, structure, HR, motivation, leadership, communication, teams, control, and synthesis

class_participation.description: Active engagement in case discussions, demonstration exercises, and structured debates throughout the semester

major_assignments.description: Three major assignments: company diagnostic (Assignment 1), motivation and leadership case analysis (Assignment 2), personal management development plan (Assignment 3)

midterm_examination.description: Written examination covering weeks 1-8: management history, the four functions, environment, ethics, decision-making, planning, strategy, structure

final_examination.description: Comprehensive written examination covering all 15 weeks, with emphasis on the second half: HR, motivation, leadership, communication, teams, control, synthesis

course_policies.attendance: Active participation in case discussions is essential to the learning model of this course. Attendance is expected at every session. Two unexcused absences are allowed; subsequent absences reduce the participation grade. Excused absences (illness, family emergency, religious observance) require advance notice when possible.
course_policies.late_submissions: Major assignments are due at the start of class on the due date. Late submissions are penalized 10% per calendar day, up to a maximum of three days, after which the assignment receives zero. Extensions may be granted for documented illness or emergency if requested in advance.
course_policies.collaboration: Quiz answers must be the student's individual work. The first major assignment (Company Diagnostic) is individual. The second (Case Analysis) may be discussed with classmates but written individually. The third (Personal Development Plan) is individual and confidential. Collaboration without acknowledgment is plagiarism.
course_policies.academic_integrity: The university's academic integrity policy applies. Use of generative AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) is permitted as a research and editing aid but not as a substitute for analysis or writing. Submitted work that is substantially AI-generated will be treated as plagiarism. When in doubt, cite the AI tool and the prompt used. Any sources used (textbooks, articles, websites, AI tools) must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop or tablet capable of accessing Harvard Business Publishing cases, the textbook, and the learning management system. Office productivity software (Word, Excel, PowerPoint or equivalents) is required for assignments. Reliable internet access is required for hybrid sessions and case research.
